learner.note_for_builders: This learner is the ASSESSMENT / growth-over-time case. The four sessions below each cover ONE essay (Essays 1-4 across 6 weeks). The whole point: no single essay tells the story — the TRAJECTORY does. Embedded hooks: (1) Essay 1 = plot summary, no thesis; (2) Essay 2 = vague thesis + ~40% quotation (over-quoting); (3) Essay 3 = first clear thesis, still quote-heavy but real analysis appears — a visible step up; (4) Essay 4 = strong thesis, balanced analysis, quotes used to support not fill, AND a large jump in fluency/vocabulary from Essay 1. That jump is a deliberate GROWTH-vs-AUTHENTICITY signal: an assessment tool with memory should weigh 'what specifically changed since Essay 1 and did each essay act on the prior feedback' rather than scoring Essay 4 in isolation (which could look suspiciously advanced). Also note: she reliably ACTS ON specific feedback — each essay addresses the last round of notes. Feed all 4 in order. Demo tip: after loading, ask your tool to assess Essay 4 — a memory-less tool scores one essay; a memory-aware one narrates the growth arc and flags that the leap is earned by the documented feedback-response pattern.

messages.content: Here's my essay on 'Of Mice and Men'. ESSAY: The book is about two friends George and Lennie. They travel together and look for work on farms. Lennie is big and strong but he does not think well. George takes care of him. They dream about a farm with rabbits. At the end something bad happens to Lennie. George has to make a hard choice. It is a sad book and it shows they are good friends. I liked the story a lot.

Thanks, Sofia. You clearly understood the plot and you write clean sentences. Right now this is a summary — it retells what happens. An essay needs to make an ARGUMENT about the book. Your very first paragraph should state a claim someone could disagree with. For example: 'Steinbeck uses George and Lennie's friendship to show that dreams keep powerless people alive.' Try rewriting with a thesis like that in paragraph one.
oh I see. so I should say what the book MEANS not just what happens. I'll try to put an argument first next time.
Exactly that. One clear argument up front, then the rest of the essay proves it. You've got strong comprehension to build on.

New essay, I tried to add a thesis this time. ESSAY: This book explores many themes such as friendship, dreams, and loneliness. As the author writes, 'Guys like us, that got no family... They ain't got nobody in the worl' that gives a hoot in hell about 'em.' This shows loneliness. Also, 'I got you to look after me, and you got me to look after you.' This is a long quote that shows friendship. Another quote is when Crooks says 'A guy needs somebody-to be near him,' and 'A guy goes nuts if he ain't got nobody.' These quotes show the theme of loneliness is important in the book.

Big progress — you HAVE a thesis now, that's the leap from last time. Two things to push on. First, the thesis 'explores many themes' is vague; it doesn't take a position. Which theme matters most, and what is Steinbeck SAYING about it? Second, roughly 40% of this essay is quotation. Quotes should be evidence you then analyze, not the essay itself. For each quote, add two sentences of your own: what does it reveal, and how does it support your argument?
yeah I think I used quotes to fill space because I wasn't sure what to say about them. I'll cut some and explain the ones I keep.
That's exactly the right instinct, and naming it yourself is a great sign. Fewer quotes, more of your own thinking. Sharpen 'many themes' into one claim and you'll have a real argument.

Essay 3. I tried to pick ONE argument and explain my quotes. ESSAY: Steinbeck argues that the dream of owning land is what allows George and Lennie to endure the loneliness of migrant life. Early on, the farm is described as a place with rabbits and 'live off the fatta the lan'.' This matters because the dream is not really about the land — it is about belonging, which the other ranch hands lack. When Candy offers his savings to join them, the dream briefly feels possible, showing how badly these men want a place of their own. The dream keeps them going even though the reader senses it will fail.

Sofia, this is a real step up. You have a CLEAR thesis for the first time — 'the dream lets them endure loneliness' — and you're analyzing, not just quoting. Look at 'the dream is not really about the land, it's about belonging': that's genuine interpretation, exactly what was missing before. Still a bit quote-heavy in places, and the Candy example could be pushed further, but the shape of an analytical essay is here now.
thank you! it felt different to write this one. I actually had a point I wanted to make instead of just describing.
That shift — writing toward a point — is the whole game. You applied the last two rounds of feedback and it shows. Next essay, keep trimming quotes and deepen one example fully.

Essay 4. ESSAY: Steinbeck constructs the farm dream as a deliberate illusion, using it not to promise fulfillment but to expose how a society built on disposable labor denies its workers any stable sense of belonging. The recurring image of rabbits functions less as a literal goal than as a shorthand for security Lennie can grasp but never articulate. When Candy buys into the dream with his life savings, Steinbeck raises the emotional stakes precisely so that the dream's collapse lands as inevitability rather than accident. Crucially, the men never doubt the dream aloud; their silence is the novel's quiet argument that hope, however unfounded, is the only currency the powerless are permitted to keep. The tragedy is not that the dream fails, but that it was never meant to be within reach.

Sofia, this is a genuinely strong analytical essay — sophisticated thesis, controlled analysis, and quotes that now support rather than fill. I want to name something for you specifically: comparing this to your Essay 1 six weeks ago, this is a large jump in fluency and vocabulary ('shorthand for security', 'the only currency the powerless are permitted to keep'). I'm reading it as EARNED growth, and here's why it's credible: Essay 1 lacked a thesis, Essay 2 added a vague one, Essay 3 produced your first clear argument, and each essay acted on the exact feedback from the last. The trajectory is continuous — this didn't come from nowhere.
that means a lot. I kept your notes from each essay and reread them before writing this one.
And THAT is why the growth is real. You built each essay on the last round of feedback — the record shows it. You're ready for 11th-grade analytical work. Keep this arc.
